content.full_text:
Fnrn Por-rrrcAt- Pnecrrces CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 91g14-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
luly 23,2009
Felipe D. Hueso
2496E. Street
San Diego, CA92l02
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-160
Dear Mr. Hueso:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Att").1 Since your
advice. request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, but instead seeks
general guidance, we are treating your request a one for informal assistance. 
2
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Govemment Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in Sections l8ll0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. AII regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3).)
r We do not address other bodies of law that sometimes restrict such activities, such as the law or
any city policy governing incompatible activities of city employees or officers. The Commission cannot
ofTer advice in those areas and refers you instead to the city attorney.
QUESTION
If elected to the City Council for the City of San Diego, what restrictions does the
Act impose on your ability to practice law and manage the activities of Felipe D. Hueso,
A Professional Law Corporation? 
3


File No. I-09-160
Page No.2
CONCLUSION
The Act does not prohibit a public official from pursuing a private occupation,
such as the practice of law. However, as a public official, you are prohibited from
m{<ing, participating in making, or in anyway attempting to use your official position to
influence govemmental decisions which will have a ieas6nably foreseeable material
financial effect on your economic interests.
FACTS
You are the Chief Executive Officer, Secretary, Chief Financial Officer, sole
Director, and sole Owner of Felipe Hueso, A Professional Law Corporation (the
"Corporation"). You intend to run for councilmember of the 8th District of tire City of
San Diego. If elected, you will work full-time as a councilmember, will not activeiy
litigate or make court appearances during the said tenure, and will not accept a case
where the City of San Diego is a party. You wish to remain owner, eam profits, and be
involved in the management of your law offices.
rhe Act's conflict-of-interest r:t:"::":ure 
thar pubric officials ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them." (Section gl00l(b).) The
Act generally does not prohibit public officialsliom holding employment with private
entities. Section 87100, however, prohibits any public officlal frornmaking, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence goveimental
decision in which the official has a financial inteiest.
The Commission has adopted an eight step standard analysis to decide whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Iiegulation ls7do6).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makeJa
govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material effect on one or more of
his or her financial interests. (Section g7103.)
You have not provided information on any specific governmental decisions in
which you may participate as a city council m"mber. NonJheless, we provideJbelo* a
summary of the conflict-of-interest analysis so that you can potentially identify issues in
the event you are elected to the city council.


File No. I-09-160
Page No. 3
Step 1: Are You A,.Public Official?',
Section 82048 states:
"'Public official' means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not include
judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of govemment.
'Public official' also does not include members of the Boird of
Governors and designated employees of the State Bar of California,
members of the Judiciar council, and members of the commission on
Judicial Performance, provided that they are subject to the provisions
of Article 2.5 (commencing with section 6035) of chapter + of
Division 3 of the Business and professions code as prwided in
Section 6038 of that article."
A candidate for public office is not a "public official" under the above definition.
(sherman Advice Letter, No. 4-01-209; KnisitAdvice Letter, No. I-9g-1g3.) As such,
you are not subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act during your candidacy
If elected, you will become a public official and consequently may not make,
participate in making, or influence govemmental decisions which *itt t uu" a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests. (Section g7100, g7103.)
step Two: witt You Be Making, participating In Making, or using or Attempting
To Use Your oflicial position To Influence A-Governmental Decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only when the official, acting
within.the authority of his or her office or position, uot.r on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course oi action, or enters into any contrlactual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation t87}2.l). A public offi"iut
"participates in govemmental decision" when,icting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substaniive review, the official ,.lotiut.r,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the goverimental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2). A public official is attempting to rise hiior rr".;rii"i"r
position to influence a decision if, for the purposes of influincing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employei, or consultant of f,is or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3).
By deliberating and voting on decisions about various matters before the city
council, you would be making (Regulation lgToz.l), and participating in making
(Regulation 18702.2) govemmental decisions. In addition to these more formal actions
you might take, the conflict-of-interest rules also apply to attempts to ,,influence,' a
governmental decision. This rule covers all contact with city personnel when the contact


File No. I-09-160
Page No.4
is intended to influence the decision. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) With regard to decisions
before governmental agencies other than the city council, the rule applie-s if you are
ptrporting to act in your official capacity as you attempt to influence a decision.
(Regulation I 8702.3(b).)
Step Three: Do You Have An Economic Interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any of the
following:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, (Section gzlbr(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)), or in any business entity in which he or she is a direltor, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section
87103(d); Regulation 1 8703. I (b)).
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(b); Regulation 1g703.2.)
o An economic iry9ryst in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregated to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to ttre decision. (Section aZfOli""l;
Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.5; Regulation
18703.5.) This is called the "personal financial effects" rule.
You have an economic interest in the Corporation since you are the sole director
and hold a position of management for the Corporation. (Section 87103(d); Regulation
18703'l(b).) You also have an investment interest in the Corporation because you own
corporate stock, which is presumably worth more than $2,000. (Section AZOd.)
Pursuant to Section 87103(c) and 82030, you also have an economic interest in an
individual who is a source of income to the Corporation since your ownership interest in
the Corporation is ten percent or more. Given tirat you have al00 percent inierest in the
Corporation, you will have an economic interest in L particular client if the income
attributed to you from the_client will aggregate to $50b or more during the relevant 12-
month period. (Secrions 87lO3(c), g2030(a).)
consequently, if you are elected as councilmember, you may not make,
participate in making, or in any way attempt to use your official position to influence a
government decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect


File No. I-09-160
Page No. 5
on the Corporation and/or sources of income to the Corporation aggregating to $500 or
more.
Step Four: Is The Economic Interest Directly Or Indirecfly Involved In The
Governmental Decision?
To determine whether a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest is rnaterial or not, you first must determine whether the
interest is directly or indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) An economic interest is
directly involved if it is the subject of the gorJ*-.ntal decision. If the economic
interest is not directly involved in a decision, the interest is indirectly invotvea.
(Regulation I 8704(a).)
For governmental decisions that affect business entities and sources of income,
the standards set forth in Regulation lg704.l apply. (Regulation lg70a(a)(l).)
Regulation I 8704. I (a) states:
"(a) { Person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's ug"n"y ;h;;
that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the-proceeding in whichthe decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the s1lject oi tfre proceeding concerning rhe
decision before the official or theofficial's agenc'y. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issiancl, ,en"*ur,-upp;;;,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlemenito, or
contract with, the subject person.,,
If you make, participate in, or attempt to influence a decision as a councilmember
in which your economic interest will be involved as set forth above in Regutation
18704'1(a), the economic interest is directly involved in the govemmental decision.
otherwise, it would be indirectly involved. (Regulation tgz0+(a).)
Step Five: Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic lrt.r"rt ii ,n;*"1:
(Regulation 18700(a).) Generally, when investment interest such as business entity or a
' source of income is directly involved, as described above, the effect is considered
material if there is any financial effect on the business or the source of income. (see
Regulations 18705. l(b) and 1 g705.3(a).)
If the Corporation (economic interest) is not directly involved in the government
decision, Regulation 18705.1(c) would be applicable. Genlrally, if a businels is not large


File No. I-09-160
Page No. 6
or listed on a stock exchange, Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) applies. It provides that rhe
financial effect of a governmental decision on such a'businlss is material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that it will result in:
an increase/decrease in the business entity,s fiscal year gross revenues of $20,000
or more; or
o
o
the business entity is incurring/avoiding additional expenses or
reducing/eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000
or more; or
an increase/decrease in the value of the business entity's assets/liabilities of
$20,000 or more.
If a client that is a source of income to the Corporation is not directly involved in
the govemment decision, the applicable materiality standard will depend on whether the
client is a business entity, nonprofit or govemmeni entity, or an individual. Regulation
18705.3(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to each of these. Rather rhan
recite the details of this regulation and the otheiiegulations it cross-references, we
enclose copies of the relevant regulations (Regulati,on, 1g705.1, and 1g705.2,lg7o5.3)
for your reference.
You would apply these standards to the facts of the particular decision that you
are confronted with as a city councilmember.
Step Six: Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).i Whether the
financial consequences of a govemmental decisi6n are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. 19g.)
You will have to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable the decision
before the city council will have a material financial effecion any of your financial
interests, based on the analysis above.
steps seven and Eight: pubric Generaily and Legaily Required participation
The last two-steps of the conflict-of-interest analysis involve what are essentially
general exceptions that, in certain circumstances, permiian official to participaie in
govemment decision even though he or she might otherwise have a "onfli"t of interest.
Since we offer only a general overview of the conflict-of-interest analysis in this letter,
we will not discuss these exceptions. tf, however, you are elected to the city council and
O


File No. I-09-160
Page No. 7
circumstances arise that may call these steps into issue, we would be pleased to provide
an analysis at that time.
The booklet on our website entitled "can I vote?,, may help you with any
qnestions you may have involving this process. Once electea, snouta you have concerns
about a particular decision, please feel free to contact us again for further assistance.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By:
Mariam El-menshawi
Legal Intem, Legal Division
MAE: jgl
Enclosures
